learn.title: Production Linked Incentive for Solar Manufacturing
learn.intro: India aims to become a global hub for solar hardware. The Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme fuels domestic factories, cuts import dependence, and creates jobs, accelerating the clean‑energy transition while keeping costs low for consumers.
learn.c1_text: Manufacturers earn a cash incentive for every megawatt‑peak (MWp) of solar cells, modules or EPC services they produce. For example, a plant making 100 MWp can receive up to ₹1.5 crore per MWp, similar to a bonus for each car a factory assembles.
learn.c2_text: The incentive is tied to quality benchmarks like ≥ 20% efficiency for cells and ≥ 18% for modules, pushing firms to adopt higher‑grade silicon and automation, which lowers per‑watt cost for projects across the grid.
learn.c3_text: Since 2022, the PLI has attracted over 70 GW of announced solar manufacturing capacity, creating 1.5 million jobs and reducing India’s solar import bill by an estimated $2 billion annually.
learn.stat1: India: 70 GW solar manufacturing capacity announced under PLI (2023)
learn.stat2: Incentive rate: up to ₹1.5 crore per MWp for modules
learn.stat3: Job creation target: 1.5 million direct jobs

learn.title: Sodium‑Ion Batteries: Affordable Alternative
learn.intro: Sodium‑ion batteries promise lower raw‑material costs and safer operation than lithium‑ion, opening pathways for large‑scale storage in emerging markets where affordability is key for renewable integration.
learn.c1_text: Sodium is 1,000 times more abundant than lithium. A typical 10 kWh sodium‑ion pack costs roughly $150/kWh versus $200/kWh for lithium, similar to choosing a common car over a premium model.
learn.c2_text: Modern sodium‑ion cells reach 90–110 Wh/kg and can operate from –20 °C to 50 °C, making them suitable for hot climates and grid‑scale use where energy density is less critical than cost and safety.
learn.c3_text: IRENA reports that by 2030, sodium‑ion could power 30 GW of stationary storage, especially in regions like Africa and South Asia where lithium supply chains are limited.
learn.stat1: Sodium abundance: ~2,600 ppm in crust vs 0.0017 ppm for lithium
learn.stat4: Energy density: 90–110 Wh/kg (comparable to early lithium cells)

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels: Power From Both Sides
learn.intro: Bifacial modules capture sunlight on the front and rear, boosting energy yield without extra land. This technology is reshaping utility‑scale farms, especially in high‑albedo environments like deserts and snowy regions.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a mirror‑backed solar panel that also harvests reflected light. In a typical 1 MW plant, bifacial modules can increase output by 10–20% compared to traditional panels, like adding an extra 0.2 MW without new space.
learn.c2_text: The rear side benefits from ground albedo (reflectivity). White sand or snow can reflect up to 80% of light, raising the bifacial gain. Modules are rated with a Bifacial Factor (BF) of 1.1–1.2, indicating 10–20% extra energy.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2 GW Bifacial Solar Park in Gujarat expects a 15% higher capacity factor, delivering roughly 3.3 TWh annually—enough for 400,000 homes—while using the same land footprint as conventional farms.
learn.stat2: Albedo of sand: up to 80% reflectivity

learn.title: The Duck Curve Challenge at Sunset
learn.intro: As solar generation peaks midday, the grid sees a steep drop at sunset while demand spikes. This “duck curve” creates operational stress, requiring flexible resources to keep lights on without over‑building infrastructure.
learn.c1_text: Picture a graph shaped like a duck: low net load at noon (the belly) and a sharp rise in the evening (the head). In California, net demand fell 30 GW below average midday, then rose 15 GW within two hours after sunset.
learn.c2_text: Fast‑ramping resources—like battery storage, demand‑response, or gas peakers—must fill the gap. A 1 GW battery can shave 5–10 GW of the evening ramp, similar to a reserve tank that releases water quickly when demand spikes.
learn.c3_text: The DOE’s “Confronting the Duck Curve” program funds 5 GW of storage projects and advanced forecasting tools, aiming to smooth the ramp and avoid costly curtailment of solar energy.
learn.stat1: Midday net load dip: 30 GW below average (CA, 2022)
learn.stat4: Battery impact: 1 GW battery reduces ramp by up to 10 GW

learn.title: Solar Inverters: String, Micro & Hybrid
learn.intro: Inverters turn DC from panels into usable AC for homes and the grid. Choosing between string, micro, or hybrid inverters affects efficiency, cost, and how well a system handles shading or storage.
learn.c1_text: All panels in a series connect to one central inverter—like a single highway toll booth. They’re cost‑effective for uniform, unshaded arrays, delivering 96‑98% efficiency at scale.
learn.c2_text: Each panel gets its own inverter, acting like a personal charger. This mitigates shading losses; a single dusty panel won’t drag down the whole string, improving energy yield by up to 5% in complex roofs.
learn.c3_text: Hybrid units combine grid‑tie and battery‑charge functions. In India, hybrid inverters enable rooftop PV with 5 kWh storage to supply night‑time power, reducing grid draw by 30% for a typical household.
learn.stat2: Micro inverter shading gain: up to 5%
learn.stat3: Hybrid rooftop PV + 5 kWh storage: 30% grid reduction
learn.stat4: Cost difference: micro ≈ 20% higher than string per kW

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells: Beyond Silicon
learn.intro: Perovskite cells promise record efficiencies and low‑temperature manufacturing, potentially surpassing silicon’s limits. Their rapid progress could shrink solar’s carbon footprint and lower balance‑of‑system costs for the next generation of farms.
learn.c1_text: These crystals absorb a broad light spectrum, similar to a sponge soaking up many colors. Lab cells have reached 26% efficiency—higher than the 24% ceiling of commercial silicon—while being printable on flexible substrates.
learn.c2_text: Perovskite layers can be deposited via roll‑to‑roll printing at < 150 °C, unlike silicon’s > 1,000 °C furnace. This reduces energy use by 70% and enables lightweight modules for building‑integrated photovoltaics.
learn.c3_text: A NREL‑India collaboration installed a 10 kW perovskite‑silicon tandem demo in Bangalore (2024), achieving 30% higher energy yield than a comparable silicon‑only system, showcasing potential for dense urban rooftops.
learn.stat4: Bangalore demo: 30% higher yield than silicon
